teaching_card.title: Punctuation and Possessives (Easy)
teaching_card.body: Possession is shown with an apostrophe: add 's to a singular noun, and just an apostrophe after a plural noun ending in -s. A plain -s makes a plural, not a possessive.
key_points: Plural ending in -s: add only an apostrophe.
No apostrophe makes a noun plural, not possessive.

screens.title: Possessive vs. plural
screens.body: An apostrophe shows possession, not a plural. Singular owner: add 's (the dog's leash). Plural owner already ending in -s: add just an apostrophe (the dogs' leashes). A plain -s with no apostrophe just makes a plural.
key_points: Singular owner: 's.  Plural owner ending in -s: s'.

screens.title: its / it's and commas
screens.body: 'It's' always means 'it is' or 'it has'; the possessive 'its' has no apostrophe. For commas, don't split a subject from its verb, and use a pair of commas around nonessential information you could lift out.
key_points: it's = it is; its = belonging to it.
Nonessential info gets a comma on both sides.

screens.title: Try the moves
screens.body: 'The teams' uniforms' = uniforms of several teams. 'The team's uniform' = one team. And 'The dog wagged its tail' takes no apostrophe — 'it's tail' would read 'it is tail.'
screens.worked_example: Workers' tools (plural owners) vs. a worker's tools (one).
